motto: Serving Greywater Falls since 1887, and the lake somewhat longer.
weather: Clear and cold, 29°F at dawn. Ice rim widened to seven inches at the south end overnight. No wind. Sky the colour of old tin. Hard factual light by midmorning.

undercurrent.note: A fifth letter — the first addressee for whom no arrival backstory has yet been sketched — is the premise's cleanest escalation yet: the series is no longer about connecting to known or semi-known names, it is generating entirely new ones. The town now has to reckon with the fact that it may be receiving mail on behalf of people who do not yet exist in any record it holds. Russ's retrieval-and-replacement of the scarf is a beautiful small beat: the lost-and-found now means something different than it did when he left the scarf there, and he intuited it without naming it. The rabbit and the drake joining Mr. Whiskers at the shore is the returned expanding beyond a single known pet — the phenomenon is widening its visible footprint. Gerald reading the sixth line aloud by accident and then asking it not be printed is the town's most intimate record-keeping instinct (shared between him and Wren) running up against something that resists documentation. The ice doubling overnight is the lake's own calendar, running faster than the town's. Undercurrent to 65, stage 2 holding, pressing toward the upper edge of stage 2.

editor.name: Wren Aldercott
editor.voice: Dry, precise, allergic to drama, which is unfortunate given recent events. Writes like a person holding a clipboard while standing in rising water. Lets one true thing slip per edition, usually in the last line.

season_arc.premise: Something is using the Gazette's lost-and-found as a postal address — and the letters it sends back are addressed to people who have not yet arrived in Greywater Falls.
